file_name: Kani et al. - 2022 - Classification of skin lesion images using modified Inception V3 model with transfer learning and au.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed a deep learning-based Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system using a modified Inception V3 model with transfer learning and data augmentation to classify dermoscopy skin lesion images (benign vs. malignant melanoma) from the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a modified Inception V3 model for skin lesion classification. It details a specific methodology including data preprocessing, transfer learning, and model architecture modifications, and reports original experimental results (e.g., 91.29% accuracy) on the ISIC dataset, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Classification of skin lesion images using modified Inception V3 model with transfer learning and augmentation techniques
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning-based Computer-Aided Diagnosis system to classify dermoscopy skin lesion images as benign or malignant melanoma using a modified Inception V3 model. The architecture combined the Inception Net with a DenseNet block for fine-grained feature extraction and utilized transfer learning with data augmentation on the ISIC dataset containing 3,307 images. The model was trained using the RMSprop optimizer with a learning rate of 0.0001 and a batch size of 64. Performance was evaluated using confusion matrices and ROC-AUC curves, yielding an accuracy of 91.29%, sensitivity of 82.87%, and specificity of 91.29%. The proposed model demonstrated superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods such as ResNet, VGG-16, and MobileNet, achieving an AUC score of 87.40%.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies skin lesions into two classes: benign and malignant melanoma. The dataset contained 1197 malignant melanoma images in the training/validation split and 300 in the testing split.

The study classifies skin lesions into two classes: benign and malignant melanoma. The dataset contained 1450 benign images in the training/validation split and 360 in the testing split. The paper refers to them generally as 'benign' without specifying sub-types like nevus or seborrheic keratosis.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesion images into benign or malignant melanoma categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper develops a Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system to classify dermoscopy skin lesion images as benign or malignant. The modified Inception V3 model outputs probabilities for two classes (benign 0 or malignant 1) using a SoftMax classifier. Performance is evaluated using confusion matrices, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and ROC-AUC curves on the ISIC dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) containing dermoscopy images of skin lesions. The study utilized a subset of 3,307 images.

use.training: Used for training the modified Inception V3 model with transfer learning and data augmentation (2118 images from the 80% split).
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (529 images from the 20% split).
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation and confusion matrix analysis (660 images).
use.other: The pre-trained Inception V3 model was originally trained on the ISIC 2019 dataset for the transfer learning phase.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the classification model by comparing predicted labels against actual labels (True Positives, False Positives, True Negatives, False Negatives) and deriving metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1 score.

statistical_methods_used.method: ROC-AUC (Receiver Operating Characteristic - Area Under Curve)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To measure the model's performance across different classification thresholds and to assess the ability to distinguish between benign and malignant classes.

To assess model generalization by splitting the dataset into training (80%), validation (20% of training set), and testing sets, and comparing performance across different optimizers (RMSprop, Adam, SGD).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC, and compared qualitatively to state-of-the-art methods.

validation.description: 20% of the 2647 images used for validation (2647 * 20%)

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained weights from ImageNet dataset; Phase I used a pre-trained Inception v3 model trained on the ISIC 2019 dataset for transfer learning.
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224x3 pixels (ISIC dataset); Default Inception V3 input is 299x299 pixels but not used in this study.

architecture_details.modifications: The final three layers of the pretrained Inception V3 model were replaced with: 1 Flatten layer, 4 Dense layers, 3 Dropout layers, and 3 Batch Normalization layers.

training_details.hardware: Google Colab TPU, HP computer (Intel i7-6700 @ 4.00 GHz, 32GB RAM), NVIDIA Jetson Nano V2 (8GB graphics card)

preprocessing_techniques: Image normalization (converting pixel values to a range between 0 and 1 by subtracting average pixel value)
Data augmentation (rotation, translation, scaling, intensity shifts, and noise addition)
Dropout (randomly dropping neurons with probability 0.2 to 0.4 during training)
Batch normalization (standardization and normalization of input layers)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to dataset images to avoid overfitting and enhance generalization capability.

conclusions: The proposed modified Inception V3 model with transfer learning and data augmentation achieved a classification accuracy of 91.29% (specifically 87.12% testing accuracy with RMSprop optimizer) for distinguishing between benign and malignant melanoma skin lesions.

The modified Inception V3 architecture, which integrates a DenseNet block for fine-grained feature extraction after flattening Inception Net features, demonstrated superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods including ResNet, VGG-16, DenseNet, MobileNet, GoogleNet, and AlexNet.

Among the tested optimizers (RMSprop, Adam, SGD), the RMSprop optimizer with a learning rate of 0.0001 yielded the best results, achieving an accuracy of 87.12%, sensitivity of 82.87%, specificity of 91.29%, and an AUC of 87.40%.

The study confirms that deep learning-based Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) systems can effectively assist dermatologists by providing objective, accurate classification of dermoscopy images, thereby addressing the subjectivity and time constraints associated with manual diagnosis.

Data augmentation techniques (rotation, translation, scaling, intensity shifts, and noise addition) were effective in enhancing the model's generalization capability and reducing overfitting on the limited ISIC dataset of 3,307 images.
The proposed model showed high specificity (91.29%), indicating a strong ability to correctly identify benign lesions, while maintaining a sensitivity of 82.87% for malignant melanoma detection.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single, relatively small dataset (3,307 images from the ISIC archive) without external validation, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the comparison between the proposed model and state-of-the-art methods; comparisons are based on point estimates of accuracy and AUC.

There is a discrepancy in reported metrics: the abstract and conclusion cite 91.29% accuracy, while the detailed results for the best-performing model (RMSprop) show 87.12% testing accuracy and 90.33% precision; the 91.29% figure appears to correspond to specificity or a different split not clearly defined in the results tables.

The dataset split is non-standard: 2,647 images were used for training/validation (80/20 split), and 660 images were reserved for testing, totaling 3,307 images. The specific source of the 660 test images relative to the ISIC 2019 dataset used for pre-training is not fully clarified, raising potential data leakage concerns if test images overlap with the pre-training set.
The 'benign' class is a heterogeneous group containing various non-melanoma skin lesions without sub-classification, which may mask performance variations across specific benign types.
Hardware specifications vary across experiments (Google Colab TPU, HP computer, NVIDIA Jetson Nano), which may introduce variability in training dynamics or reproducibility.

The model achieves high specificity (91.29%) but lower sensitivity (82.87%), indicating a potential bias towards identifying benign lesions and potentially missing malignant cases, which is critical in clinical screening contexts.
